message.content:
Adversarial refuter: ASSUME this finding is FALSE; disprove it from the code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. survives=true only if you genuinely cannot.
P7-PII-1: Operator full display name (git author) is NOT scrubbed — leaks in committed transcripts (CV-O-1 class)
scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38
The transcript scrubber (_SECRET_PATTERNS + _USERNAME_RE) redacts the operator's EMAIL and the bare home-directory USERNAME ('[REDACTED]'), and masks the username inside /Users//home paths and project-dir slugs. It does NOT redact the operator's real full DISPLAY NAME ('Thomas Jones'), which is distinct operator PII that appears throughout the transcripts via git author strings, pyproject authors=[], and prose. This is exactly the same coverage-gap class as pass-6's CV-O-1 (a category of sensitive content that bypasses redaction). The leak is real and already committed: 'Thomas Jones' is present in cleartext in 5+ files under transcripts/sessions/ (e.g. the top-level .jsonl and several subagent transcripts), frequently sitting directly next to a successfully-redacted email, e.g. `{ name = "Thomas Jones", email = "[REDACTED]" }` and `Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>`. De-identifying the email while leaving the adjacent full name defeats the de-identification (the whole point of #F-024/#G-007 was to avoid publishing the operator's identity). git config user.name = 'Thomas Jones' confirms this is the operator's identity, not a coincidental token. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers full-name redaction, confirming the gap is unintentional rather than a documented scope decision.
PoC: 1) cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
2) grep -rc 'Thomas Jones' transcripts/  -> multiple files with count >=1 (cleartext)
3) python3 -c 'import importlib.util as u; s=u.spec_from_file_location("c","scripts/capture_transcripts.py"); m=u.module_from_spec(s); s.loader.exec_module(m); print(m._scrub("Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>"))'
   -> prints: Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>   (email masked, full name leaks)
4) git config user.name -> 'Thomas Jones' (confirms it is operator PII)

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll investigate this finding about the operator's full display name not being scrubbed.

content.content:
1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import re
26	import sys
27	from pathlib import Path
28	
29	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
34	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
35	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
36	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
37	# for the full public-release consideration.
38	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
39	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
40	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
41	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
42	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
43	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
44	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
45	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
46	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
47	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
48	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
49	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
50	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
51	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
53	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
54	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
55	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
56	]
57	
58	
59	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
60	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
61	
62	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
63	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
64	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
65	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
66	    """
67	    try:
68	        name = Path.home().name
69	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
70	        return None
71	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
72	        return None
73	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
74	
75	
76	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
77	
78	
79	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
80	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
81	        if pat.groups == 2:
82	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
83	        elif pat.groups == 1:
84	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
85	        else:
86	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
87	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
88	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
89	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
90	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
91	    return text
92	
93	
94	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
95	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
96	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
97	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
98	
99	
100	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
101	    try:
102	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
103	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
104	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
105	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
106	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
107	        # Decode strictly to classify: text → scrub; genuinely binary (rare here) → copy verbatim.
108	        try:
109	            text = raw.decode("utf-8")
110	        except UnicodeDecodeError:
111	            data = raw  # binary blob — nothing to scrub
112	        else:
113	            data = _scrub(text).encode("utf-8")
114	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
115	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
116	        else:
117	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
118	        return {
119	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
120	            "bytes": len(data),
121	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
122	        }
123	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
124	        return None
125	
126	
127	def main() -> int:
128	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
129	    if not src.is_dir():
130	        return 0
131	    session = _active_session(src)
132	    if session is None:
133	        return 0
134	    sid = session.stem
135	    captured: list[dict] = []
136	
137	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
138	    if rec:
139	        captured.append(rec)
140	
141	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
142	    subtree = src / sid
143	    if subtree.is_dir():
144	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
145	            if f.is_file():
146	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
147	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
148	                if rec:
149	                    captured.append(rec)
150	
151	    try:
152	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
153	        MANIFEST.write_text(
154	            json.dumps(
155	                {
156	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
157	                    "active_session": sid,
158	                    "n_files": len(captured),
159	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
160	                    "files": captured,
161	                },
162	                indent=2,
163	            )
164	            + "\n",
165	            encoding="utf-8",
166	        )
167	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
168	        return 0
169	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
170	    return 0
171	
172	
173	if __name__ == "__main__":
174	    sys.exit(main())
175

content.text:
All key claims verified:

1. **Scrubber leaves "Thomas Jones" intact** — `_scrub("Author: Thomas Jones <email>")` returns `'Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>'`. The full display name is NOT redacted while the adjacent email IS. Confirmed.

2. **"Thomas Jones" is in cleartext in committed transcripts** — grep shows 20+ files including the top-level `.jsonl` (count 5) and many subagent transcripts.

3. **It is the operator's real identity** — `git log` shows commit author `Thomas Jones <...>`. (Though `git config user.name` returned empty in this sandbox, the commit history proves the author name is "Thomas Jones".)

4. **No test covers full-name redaction** — `tests/test_scripts.py` only tests the username handle (`[REDACTED]`), not the display name "Thomas Jones".

5. **The patterns confirm the gap** — `_SECRET_PATTERNS` and `_USERNAME_RE` (`re.compile('[REDACTED]')`) target email and the lowercase concatenated handle, but nothing matches the spaced display name "Thomas Jones".

The only minor inaccuracy in the finding is the pyproject claim: line 13 actually reads `authors = [{ name = "thehipsterciso" }]`, not `{ name = "Thomas Jones" }`. So that specific example is wrong, but it does not affect the core finding — the leak is demonstrated via git author strings and prose in the transcripts.

This matches the CV-O-1 class exactly: a category of sensitive content (operator's real full name) bypasses redaction, and it is already committed in cleartext.

I attempted to disprove the finding and could not. The de-identification masks the email but leaves the adjacent real full name throughout committed transcripts.

input.reasoning: Verified and could not disprove. The scrubber in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py redacts the operator email and the bare lowercase username handle ('[REDACTED]', via _USERNAME_RE = re.compile('[REDACTED]')) but does NOT redact the spaced full display name 'Thomas Jones'. Direct PoC: _scrub("Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>") returns 'Author: Thomas Jones <[REDACTED]>' — email masked, full name leaks. grep -rc 'Thomas Jones' transcripts/ shows it in cleartext in 20+ committed files, including the top-level session .jsonl (count 5) and many subagent/workflow transcripts, frequently adjacent to a successfully-redacted email. git log shows commit author 'Thomas Jones <...>', confirming it is operator PII, not a coincidental token. No test in tests/test_scripts.py covers display-name redaction (line 88 only tests the concatenated handle, not the spaced name), confirming the gap is unintentional. This is the same coverage-gap class as pass-6 CV-O-1. Minor correction to the finding text: pyproject.toml:13 actually reads authors = [{ name = "thehipsterciso" }], not 'Thomas Jones' — so that one example is inaccurate, but it does not affect the core finding, which is independently demonstrated via git author strings and transcript prose. The leak is real and committed.

content.text: Finding survives. The operator's full display name "Thomas Jones" bypasses redaction and is committed in cleartext across 20+ transcript files, while the adjacent email is properly redacted.
